**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I write with a heart heavy with regret and the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have been meaning to write, but the sea has been a cruel and unyielding mistress, and I have been too afraid to put pen to paper, lest I be overcome by the memories that come with each wave.

You were always the light in my life, Eliza—bright, sharp, and unafraid. I remember the day you left, how you stood at the threshold of our home, your eyes full of fire and your voice full of resolve. “You’ll always be my father,” you said. “But I must go, and I will not come back.” And so you went, and I never saw you again.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, as I promised, though the loneliness has grown like the tide, creeping in and swallowing me whole. The sea has been my only companion, and I have grown used to its whispers and its warnings. But tonight, the storm is coming—a tempest like none I have seen in my time. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sky is black as a grave.

I do not know if I shall survive it, Eliza. The light must be kept burning, for the sailors who depend on it. I

My Dearest Eliza,

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my heart. I write to you now, not with the hope of a reply, but with the certainty of a final farewell. Five years have passed since our last words crossed, and I have been a solitary sentinel on this rock, watching the tides and the stars, yet never the face of my own child.

The lighthouse has stood for over a century, weathering tempests and calm, but tonight, the heavens themselves seem to conspire against us. The waves crash against the cliffs with a violence that shakes the very bones of this old tower. I have lit the lamp, though the flame flickers like a dying ember, and I fear that even it may be extinguished before dawn.

I have always believed that the sea holds secrets, and that the storms are the voices of the earth, but tonight, I am not certain if I will be the one to hear them. I have sent my last meal to the village, and I have prepared the small grave that awaits me should the worst come to pass.

I have not forgotten you, Eliza. I have thought of you often, though the silence between us has grown like the moss upon the stone. I have kept your letters, folded in the drawer beneath the lamp, and sometimes, when the wind was still and the sea calm, I would read them again

The world has moved on, and I have not. The storm is coming tonight, a tempest like none I've seen in my years here. The waves are said to rise like mountains, and the wind howls like the cries of the damned. I have not slept in days, and I do not know if I will live to see dawn. But I must write, for I have something to say before I am gone.

You needed a world, a life, a future that was not bound by the stones and the tides.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this letter with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. It is now well past the hour when the storm should be upon us, and the wind howls like a banshee at my door. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this tempest gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my dear Eleanor, my only child.

I know you have not answered my letters, and I do not blame you. You were a child of fire and spirit, always yearning for more than this quiet, steadfast life I have chosen. You wanted the world, and I, perhaps, was too afraid to give it to you. I was a lighthouse keeper, a man of routine and duty, and I thought that was enough. But you were not content with the shore, the sea, or the quiet of the night. You wanted adventure, and I could not offer it.

You left, as I had feared you would. You left in a huff, with your mother's words echoing in your ears, and I was left with only the sound of the waves and the silence of my own heart. I have not spoken to you since that day, and I do not know if I should now. But as this storm approaches, I feel something within me stir—a need to say what I have not said in five long years.

I have always loved you, Eleanor.

My Dearest Eliza,

I am no stranger to storms, but this one... this one feels as if the heavens themselves are tearing apart.

I have prepared the lantern, and I have secured the ropes. I have lit the oil lamp, though it is not the same as your voice. I have not yet lit the beacon, for I do not know if the storm will allow me to. But should I perish, know this: the light will remain, and the sea will find its way to shore. The lighthouse is a promise, and I have made mine to the world.
